claimed_contribution: The paper provides evidence that consumers form affect-based initial impressions of brand extensions from the affect elicited by the core brand name.
It shows that affect experienced for unrelated reasons can be misattributed to the brand and then influence extension evaluations when the brand is affect-eliciting.

It identifies a boundary condition for prior fit-mediation results: affect influences evaluations through perceived core-extension similarity mainly when consumers are explicitly asked to judge similarity before evaluating the extension.
context: The studies examine hypothetical brand extension judgments in consumer goods and services using familiar brands (airlines, beverages, electronics, and sportswear). Data come from university student participants in an Asian university setting.
design_and_data: Three laboratory experiments with student samples tested brand extension evaluations using factorial designs and pretested stimuli. Experiment 1 (n = 120; 2 x 2 x 2) crossed core brand favorableness, affect-eliciting versus non-affect-eliciting brands, and moderately similar versus dissimilar extensions using airline brands. Experiment 2 (n = 130; 2 x 2 x 2) induced positive or negative mood via autobiographical recall, varied whether mood was induced before or after brand exposure, and used an affect-eliciting brand (Pepsi) versus a non-affect brand (Bonaqua) with a dissimilar extension. Experiment 3 (n = 248; 2 x 2 x 2 x 2, plus a supplementary n = 80 study) manipulated induced mood, extension similarity, judgment order (similarity-first vs evaluation-first), and brand replication (Panasonic/Adidas).
main_findings: When the core brand elicited affect, extension evaluations were more favorable under positive than negative affect regardless of whether extensions were moderately similar or dissimilar; when the core brand did not elicit affect, brand favorableness mattered mainly for moderately similar extensions.

Induced mood affected extension evaluations only when mood was induced before exposure to an affect-eliciting brand, and not when mood was induced after brand exposure or when the brand was non-affect-eliciting.

When similarity was judged before evaluation, affect influenced evaluations through perceived core-extension similarity (for moderately similar, not clearly dissimilar, extensions), but without a prior similarity judgment affect influenced evaluations regardless of similarity; supplementary data with very similar extensions showed the same order-based pattern.
one_line_summary: Across three experiments, the paper shows that affect elicited by a core brand can directly shape brand extension evaluations via early affect-based impressions, while fit-based mediation appears mainly when consumers are prompted to judge similarity first.
relation_to_prior_work: refines categorization-based and fit-based accounts of brand extension evaluation by showing a parallel affect-impression route that can operate independently of perceived fit. It also reconciles prior evidence that affect works through similarity by showing that this mediation is strongest when similarity is explicitly assessed before evaluation.
research_gap: Prior brand extension research largely emphasized perceived fit and categorization, and affect effects were often treated as operating through fit perceptions. The authors argue that the role of brand-elicited affect itself, distinct from global brand attitude, had not been fully explored and may explain successful low-fit extensions seen in the marketplace.
research_question: Do affective reactions elicited by a core brand directly influence evaluations of its extensions independent of core-extension similarity, or are affect effects mediated by perceived fit? Under what conditions does affect operate through initial brand-based impressions versus similarity judgments, including when affect is misattributed from unrelated mood?
